ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03302114 represents samosa as a general category; the vegetable-filled variant with potato and pea filling is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers samosa as a general pastry category. The specific vegetable-filled variant is not individually coded; closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for samosa or vegetable samosa as a food concept. Omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 2 samosas, each approximately 50–60 g, totalling roughly 110 g, as typically served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Samosa (vegetable) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) reports values for vegetable samosa (aloo-matar filling, deep-fried). Values are per 100 g edible portion. The restaurant version at Jewel of India may differ slightly in oil absorption and spice composition; values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN reference as the closest approved primary source for this Indian dish. Secondary cross-check performed against USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Samosa, vegetable' (FDC ID 2103689) to confirm plausibility of macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplementary data for wheat-flour pastry with potato filling
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetable samosa was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) wheat-flour pastry shell (GI ~55–60 for refined wheat products per Sydney GI Database), (2) potato and pea filling (boiled potato GI ~56–78; green peas GI ~48), and (3) deep-frying which slows gastric emptying and moderates glycaemic response. A composite GI of 55 (low category) is consistent with published estimates for similar Indian fried wheat-flour pastries with starchy vegetable fillings. Glycemic load calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 55 × 34.3 / 100 ≈ 18.9 (serving size 110 g, carbohydrate per serving = 31.2 × 1.10 = 34.3 g).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~341 mg per 2-piece serving) and deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit samosa consumption to occasional servings and avoid pairing with high-sodium chutneys, as the combined sodium load from the pastry and condiments can contribute meaningfully to daily sodium targets of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~55) and moderate glycemic load (~18.9 per 2-piece serving) from refined wheat flour and potato filling
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat vegetable samosa as an occasional snack rather than a staple, pairing it with a low-GI accompaniment such as raita or salad to blunt the postprandial glucose response; portion control to one piece is advisable for tighter glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat content (~13.8 g per 100 g) from deep-frying, though saturated fat is relatively low (~2.1 g per 100 g) and cholesterol is negligible
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, vegetable samosa is preferable to meat-filled or ghee-heavy pastries due to its low saturated fat and zero cholesterol profile, but the high total fat from deep-frying warrants limiting intake to one piece per occasion within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable samosa is a classic Indian deep-fried savoury pastry made from a thin, crisp wheat-flour shell encasing a spiced filling of mashed potato and green peas. The filling is typically seasoned with cumin, coriander, garam masala, green chilli, and fresh coriander leaves, giving it a warm, aromatic flavour profile with mild heat. The pastry is folded into a triangular or cone shape and deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crunchy. It is traditionally served with tamarind chutney, which adds a sweet-sour counterpoint to the savoury filling. In Singapore, vegetable samosa is a popular snack and appetiser at Indian vegetarian restaurants, particularly those serving North Indian cuisine. A standard restaurant serving is two pieces totalling approximately 110 g. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides approximately 262 kcal, 5.2 g protein, 13.8 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from frying oil), and 31.2 g carbohydrate with 3.1 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low category), making it a moderate glycaemic food. Sodium content is approximately 310 mg per 100 g, which is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable, containing no animal products or dairy. It contains gluten from the wheat-flour pastry shell and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a veg samosa?
What is the GI of samosa?
How much fat is in a samosa?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017), the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central for plausibility. The GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement for vegetable samosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The restaurant-specific version may differ in oil absorption and spice composition. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are per 100 g edible portion, adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) for vegetable samosa (aloo-matar, deep-fried). 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food item; vitamin D is not a characteristic nutrient of deep-fried wheat-flour pastries. 3. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food item. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and consistent with the use of vegetable frying oil rather than partially hydrogenated fats. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish — vitamin B12 is naturally zero in foods of exclusively plant origin. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour pastry shell and potato-pea filling characteristics; no direct GI measurement for vegetable samosa was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The gi_category has been corrected to 'low' as per standard GI classification for a value of 55. 8. Serving size of 110 g (2 pieces) is based on typical restaurant portion for 2-piece samosa serving as described in the input record. 9. The cuisine field has been normalised from the restaurant name string in the input to 'Indian' as the canonical cuisine type. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302114 and FoodEx2 code A0FKP are used as closest-parent matches; no exact ontology code for vegetable samosa exists in either system.
